mnemonics.title: Process States Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: NWRWT - New, Waiting, Running, Waiting, Terminated (Actually: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated) - Remember: Nice Rabbits Wait Till Finished (New Ready Waiting Terminated)

mnemonics.title: PCB Contents Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: PCB contains: PID (Identity), PC (Execution pointer), Registers (CPU state), Memory (MMU info), I/O (Device status), Scheduling (Priority/queue), Accounting (CPU time used), Files (Open files list) - Memory Aid: P-RIMA-SAF - Process ID, Registers, I/O Status, Memory Info, Account Info, Scheduling Info, Accessed Files

mnemonics.title: Context Switch Steps
mnemonics.content: Remember CONTEXT: Capture current state, Organize in PCB, Notate next instruction, Transfer to new process, Switch to new process, Save old state, Execute new process, Check for interrupts, Turn to next task

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing all process information in kernel memory
Register storing address of next instruction to execute
The main processor that executes process instructions
Unique number assigned to each process in the system
Scheduling algorithm that prioritizes shortest processes

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle with 5 states (New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated). Use arrows to show transitions: New→Ready, Ready→Running (by Dispatcher), Running→Ready (preemption), Running→Waiting (I/O), Waiting→Ready (I/O complete), Running→Terminated, Ready→Terminated (can happen). Color-code: Green=admission, Red=termination, Yellow=CPU transitions.

Think of PCB as a 'passport' for each process. It has: Photo (Process ID), Current page (Program Counter), Visa stamps (CPU Registers), Address book (Memory management info), Travel history (Accounting), Access cards (I/O status), Priority card (Scheduling info), Folder list (Open files). All stored securely in kernel territory.

Imagine two people sharing one notebook. When Person A finishes their turn, they close the notebook, save what page they were on (PCB save), and Person B opens it to their marked page (PCB restore). The notebook is the CPU, the page marks are PCBs.

analogies.analogy: A program is like a recipe book (passive), while a process is someone actually cooking following the recipe (active). Multiple cooks can use the same recipe book simultaneously.

PCB is like a teacher's register for each student. It tracks: Student ID (PID), Last page read (PC), Homework answers (Registers), Seat number (Memory), Homework status (I/O), Class rank (Scheduling), Attendance (Accounting), Books borrowed (Files).
Context switch is like a teacher collecting one student's test and giving the test paper to another student. The first student's work is saved (PCB save), new student's work begins (PCB restore).
Process states are like students in a classroom: New = just admitted, Ready = sitting at desk waiting, Running = answering on board, Waiting = going to bathroom, Terminated = left school.
A zombie process is like a student who has finished exams (terminated) but the teacher hasn't collected their answer sheet yet. They stay in the classroom until the teacher collects it (wait() call).
